impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on exclusive purchase of unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act, and whether the defense that the tied products are 'nonstaple goods' lacking substantial non-infringing uses shields the patent holder from antitrust liability.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act for conditioning licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' exclusive purchase of Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the district court's determination that whether the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses was a jury question, and affirmed the corresponding damages award. The patent had separately been invalidated under § 102(g).
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors facing exclusionary tying arrangements by patent holders now have strong appellate support for antitrust claims, particularly where tied products have uses outside the patent's scope.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who bundle licenses with exclusive purchasing requirements for unpatented products face significant antitrust liability risk, including damages, even if the patent is later invalidated. Companies must carefully evaluate whether tied products have substantial non-infringing uses before imposing such conditions.

impacts.caseName: Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to methods for detecting anomalous program executions by comparing function calls in an emulator to a model of expected function calls are abstract and ineligible for patenting under 35 U.S.C. § 101, and whether damages for patent infringement may be based on foreign sales.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million jury verdict for Columbia, holding that the asserted claims of the '322 and '115 patents directed to detecting anomalous program executions (e.g., virus detection) are abstract at Alice step one and require further proceedings on step two. The court also held that the district court erred in permitting damages based on Norton's foreign sales, and reversed the contempt order against Norton's counsel Quinn Emanuel that had supported enhanced damages and attorneys' fees.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies defending against cybersecurity-related patent claims gain support for § 101 eligibility challenges. The foreign sales ruling limits patentees' ability to recover damages on overseas revenue, protecting multinational businesses from inflated damages awards.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the software and cybersecurity space face heightened risk that their claims will be deemed abstract under § 101. Universities and research institutions monetizing software patents may see reduced damages potential where accused infringers have significant foreign operations.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming methods and systems for a server to tailor content specifications to a wireless device's capabilities and deliver the content for rendering are ineligible for patenting under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held on cross-appeal that GoTV's patents relating to server-side tailoring of content to wireless device capabilities are ineligible under § 101, reversing the district court's judgment of infringement and $2.5 million damages award in favor of GoTV on the '715 patent. The court reversed the finding of indefiniteness on the '865 patent but concluded all claims at issue were patent-ineligible, requiring entry of judgment for Netflix.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming and content delivery companies gain a significant defense against patent assertions related to adaptive content delivery to mobile devices, as such claims face strong § 101 eligibility challenges.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders asserting claims directed to content adaptation and delivery technologies face a heightened bar to enforceability, narrowing the scope of enforceable software-related patents in the streaming industry.

impacts.caseName: Apple Inc. v. International Trade Commission
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC correctly found that Apple's importation and sale of Apple Watch models with blood oxygen measurement features violated § 337 of the Tariff Act by infringing Masimo's patents covering wearable pulse oximetry devices.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the ITC's finding that Apple violated § 337 by importing Apple Watch models that infringed Masimo's patents on wearable blood oxygen measurement technology, upholding the limited exclusion order barring importation of infringing devices. The court found no error in the ITC's domestic industry determination, validity rulings, or infringement findings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in the wearable health technology space receive strong validation that ITC exclusion orders remain a powerful enforcement tool against major importers of infringing consumer electronics.
impacts.negativeImplications: Major consumer electronics companies face real risk that ITC exclusion orders can block importation of flagship products. Apple must redesign its Watch blood oxygen features or license Masimo's patents to continue selling affected models in the U.S.

impacts.caseName: Ascendis Pharma A/S v. BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an ITC respondent that missed the statutory deadline for seeking a mandatory stay under 28 U.S.C. § 1659(a)(2) may obtain such a stay by voluntarily dismissing and refiling the declaratory judgment action.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that BioMarin could not obtain a mandatory stay of Ascendis's refiled declaratory judgment action under § 1659(a)(2) after having missed the deadline for a stay in the original action. The court prevented an ITC respondent from circumventing the statutory timing requirements through tactical refiling, preserving Ascendis's ability to litigate its safe harbor defense in district court concurrently with the ITC proceeding.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies facing parallel ITC and district court proceedings retain access to district court litigation when the opposing party misses the statutory stay deadline, preventing gamesmanship through dismissal and refiling.
impacts.negativeImplications: ITC respondents who fail to timely seek a mandatory stay cannot cure the missed deadline by engineering a new district court filing, reducing their strategic flexibility in managing parallel proceedings.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact precluded summary judgment on inequitable conduct in obtaining patents related to coiled tubing manufacturing, and on a Walker Process fraud claim for attempted monopolization based on that inequitable conduct.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both inequitable conduct (granted in favor of Global Tubing) and Walker Process fraud (granted in favor of Tenaris), finding genuine disputes of material fact on both issues. The case involves whether Tenaris withheld prior art (the CYMAX documents) from the PTO when prosecuting patents on its BlueCoil coiled tubing product, and whether that conduct rose to the level of fraud supporting an antitrust claim.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies accused of inequitable conduct in patent prosecution benefit from the court's insistence on genuine fact-finding before such serious findings are imposed. Walker Process fraud claimants must also meet a high factual bar.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who acquired prior art through asset acquisitions face continued litigation risk if they failed to disclose that prior art during prosecution, with potential antitrust (Walker Process) liability remaining in play.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent damages expert report under Daubert and granting summary judgment of no remedy based on the absence of damages evidence.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report and vacated the summary judgment for Microsoft based on absence of a remedy. The court found the district court applied too rigid a standard in excluding the expert's opinions, effectively denying Exafer the opportunity to present its case on patent infringement damages for patents relating to virtual network optimization technology.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent plaintiffs gain assurance that district courts cannot use overly stringent Daubert standards to effectively dismiss infringement cases by eliminating all damages evidence, preserving the right to a damages trial.
impacts.negativeImplications: Accused infringers may face longer and costlier litigation as damages experts' reports survive Daubert challenges more frequently, reducing the availability of early summary disposition.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a means-plus-function 'control means' limitation in a patent claim directed to crop harvester height control apparatus was indefinite for failure to disclose sufficient corresponding structure in the specification.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's finding that the asserted claims of the '395 patent were invalid as indefinite, holding that the specification adequately disclosed the corresponding structure for the 'control means' limitation. The court found that the specification's description of the controller interface, head controller, and hydraulic control system, even though described as 'conventional,' provided sufficient structure to inform a person of ordinary skill.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders with means-plus-function claims benefit from a more forgiving approach to corresponding structure disclosure, particularly where specifications describe components as 'conventional in design and operation.'
impacts.negativeImplications: Accused infringers lose an indefiniteness defense where the specification describes corresponding structure with references to conventional components, potentially broadening the scope of enforceable means-plus-function claims.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Board governing discretionary denials of inter partes review institution are 'general statements of policy' exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b).
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions governing discretionary non-institution of IPR proceedings are general statements of policy exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking. The court held that because Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution, and the Director retains ultimate authority over institution decisions, the challenged instructions do not have the binding force of law requiring formal rulemaking.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's retained flexibility to decline IPR institution, as the Director's guidance on discretionary denials remains insulated from procedural challenges.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies and other frequent IPR petitioners lose a significant challenge to the PTO's ability to deny institution without formal rulemaking, reducing their ability to shape IPR institution standards through the administrative process.

impacts.caseName: US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC v. Google LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to web conferencing systems with time-shifting capabilities enabling asynchronous viewing of data streams are ineligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's dismissal for failure to state a claim, holding that the '637 patent's claims relating to web conferencing with time-shifting and asynchronous playback capabilities were patent-ineligible under § 101. This continues the trend of invalidating software patents that claim conventional computing functions applied to known concepts like recording and playback.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies offering video conferencing and collaboration tools gain further protection against patent assertions based on fundamental recording and playback functionalities.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with claims directed to web conferencing and media time-shifting face an increasingly difficult path to enforceability under § 101, further narrowing the scope of enforceable software patents.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the International Trade Commission's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in countervailing duty and antidumping investigations is consistent with the governing statute and the common law right of access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of International Trade's ruling that the ITC's blanket practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential is incompatible with 19 U.S.C. §§ 1516a and 1677f. The court held that the statute does not abrogate the common law right of access to judicial records and that the ITC must make individualized confidentiality determinations consistent with the statutory framework.
impacts.positiveImplications: Importers, domestic industries, and the public gain greater transparency in trade remedy proceedings as the ITC can no longer automatically shield all questionnaire responses from disclosure, enabling better access to information underlying trade dispute decisions.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies submitting business-sensitive information in ITC questionnaires face increased risk of public disclosure. The ITC must now undertake individualized confidentiality reviews, potentially increasing administrative burden and costs for all parties.

impacts.caseName: National Veterans Legal Services Program v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly approved a $125 million class action settlement of claims that the federal judiciary charged excessive PACER fees beyond what was necessary to operate the electronic records system, and whether incentive awards to class representatives were permissible.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's approval of a $125 million settlement in a nationwide class action alleging that the federal judiciary overcharged PACER users by using fees to fund expenses unrelated to PACER's operation. The court upheld the settlement terms, attorneys' fee awards, and incentive awards to the three nonprofit class representatives, rejecting the sole objector's challenges regarding jurisdiction, settlement fairness, and the propriety of incentive awards.
impacts.positiveImplications: Law firms and businesses that are heavy PACER users will receive refunds for fees paid during the eight-year class period. The decision validates the use of incentive awards for class representatives in federal class actions.

impacts.caseName: 112 Genesee Street, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether restaurants that applied for but did not receive Restaurant Revitalization Fund grants have a money-mandating claim under the Tucker Act where the statute directed the SBA to award grants in the order applications were received.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of Federal Claims' denial of the Government's motion to dismiss, holding that over 300 restaurants plausibly stated a Tucker Act claim for unpaid RRF grants. The court found that the statute's mandatory language—directing the SBA to 'award grants' in the order applications were received—created a money-mandating obligation sufficient for jurisdiction, even though Congress subsequently rescinded unobligated RRF funds.
impacts.positiveImplications: Small businesses denied statutory grant funds due to agency processing errors gain a viable path to recover damages through Tucker Act claims, establishing that mandatory statutory grant language can create enforceable payment obligations.
impacts.negativeImplications: The federal government faces potential significant monetary liability for failing to administer grant programs in the order mandated by statute, which could affect how agencies prioritize and process applications in future relief programs.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Board correctly construed the claim term 'encryption information' in a patent covering systems for streaming partly encrypted media content, and whether the challenged claims would have been obvious over the prior art under the correct construction.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the Board's claim construction and vacated its decision upholding the patentability of DivX's '588 patent claims in inter partes review. The court adopted a different construction of a key claim term related to encryption information in streaming media systems and remanded for the Board to reassess Netflix's obviousness challenge under the corrected construction.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming technology companies challenging patents on encrypted media delivery benefit from a corrected claim construction that may broaden the universe of invalidating prior art.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the digital media encryption space face renewed invalidity challenges when appellate courts adopt broader claim constructions on remand.

impacts.caseName: Willis Electric Co., Ltd. v. Polygroup Ltd. (Macao Commercial Offshore)
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether substantial evidence supported the jury's finding that claims of a patent directed to pre-lit artificial trees with integrated mechanical and electrical connections were not invalid as obvious, and whether the damages award was supported.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's denial of Polygroup's motions for JMOL of obviousness and a new trial on damages regarding Willis's '186 patent covering pre-lit artificial tree technology with integrated trunk connectors. The court upheld the jury's finding of non-obviousness and the damages verdict, sustaining Willis's patent enforcement in the consumer products space.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in the consumer products industry receive strong affirmation that jury verdicts on non-obviousness and damages will be upheld on appeal when supported by substantial evidence, providing enforcement certainty.
impacts.negativeImplications: Manufacturers of competing consumer products face continued exposure to patent infringement damages when they cannot establish obviousness as a matter of law, particularly where prior art combinations are disputed.
